source.title: Impact of controllability on pain and suffering

confidence_rationale: Behavioural dissociation study demonstrating that controllability selectively reduces pain-related suffering while leaving pain intensity and unpleasantness unaffected. This three-way dissociation (intensity / unpleasantness / suffering) is directly relevant to Q-036 because it empirically distinguishes suffering as a distinct construct with its own determinants -- controllability being one of them -- beyond intensity and unpleasantness. Moderate source quality (Pain Reports, not a top-tier journal) but clean dissociation design. Locus of control (chance attribution) was the individual-difference predictor; catastrophising and fear showed no effect.
failure_signatures: No neural data; the mechanism by which controllability selectively reduces suffering (not intensity) is not directly observed.

Physiological indicators (skin conductance, heart rate) showed no group differences, suggesting the effect is cognitive-emotional rather than autonomic -- this limits the mapping to a REE system with autonomic coupling (SD-032e).
The study used a brief experimental pain paradigm (not chronic or persistent), so temporal accumulation and recovery failure effects are not tested.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Controllability selectively reduces pain-related suffering as a distinct dimension, without affecting pain intensity or unpleasantness. Chance-attributional locus of control predicts suffering magnitude; catastrophising and fear do not.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-036 asks what additional variables beyond temporal integration determine z_harm_a as a distinct load state. Loffler et al. show suffering is a separable dimension from intensity and unpleasantness, and that controllability is its primary modulator. In REE terms, z_harm_a as the affective harm stream should encode not just accumulated harm signal magnitude but also an estimate of controllability/escapability. This is a functionally distinct variable from what MECH-219 (temporal integrator alone) captures.
mapping.mapping_caveat: REE does not yet have an explicit representation of 'controllability belief' as a latent variable feeding z_harm_a. The finding argues that such a representation is needed for z_harm_a to function as specified (as a genuinely distinct urgency state vs a scaled version of z_harm_s). This is a design gap, not evidence against the claim.
mapping.source_context: Human behavioural study; cold pressor and pressure pain; controlled/uncontrollable conditions; individual differences in locus of control.

confidence_components.notes: Pain Reports is a lower-tier venue; the three-way dissociation is clean but no neural data. Mapping fidelity high because the suffering vs intensity dissociation directly addresses the question of whether non-temporal variables are load-determining.
